speaker: interviewer
text: I'm conducting an interview with Bluma Doman. Today is the 18th of February, 1996. The interview is being conducted in Johannesburg, South Africa, in English-- tape four. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You mentioned that you had heard Hans Biebow when you first arrived at the Lodz Ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I heard him on the-- on that place where we been staying. I heard him. I saw him briefly-- only his bald head. He talked with a lot of hate. And he wasn't our friend. This-- this you could say for sure. In Lodz ghetto, there was Rumkowski. He was the one-- people blamed him for a lot of things. But I don't think that you could help, because he was a children's lover. Before the war, he was-- he had all the orphanage in-- in Lodz. And he loved children, OK. In fact, that's mine brother got into a shop where he was fed differently and had different things for him. Rumkowski-- I never saw him. I heard of him, or I heard his voice when he was speaking. But personally, I never saw him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we're going to the-- it's OK? We're going now-- you're asking me about hate. The-- who loved the Jew, especially in that war? There was hate in everyone. There were a lot of soldiers-- Germans-- which understood Polish, you see? Because they were volksdeutsche. But to beat us, they were always there. Are you able in any way to compare Ozorkow to Lodz ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz ghetto was more deadly. Ozorkow ghetto-- we could help ourselves a little bit to food. We had-- even Poles, some of them, which brought us food. Because they knew us, you see? So it-- it-- you can't compare it to Lodz ghetto. Lodz ghetto was just one dead place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever lose faith?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been young. I tell you the truth, I never thought of death-- that's we'll survive. I only hoped that I'll meet my father somewhere alive. He was 48 years old, good looking, hands-- gold. But hate-- sure, we hate the Germans. But we were helpless. We couldn't do nothing!

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, in 1944, when I came back with this bread, I said to my brother, Arieh, you have to go. There's no word. He said, all right. You say we go, we go.

speaker: interviewee
text: We come to a place where I found my mother's-- one of the sisters. She grabbed me, but I don't know what happened. She didn't-- I just said hello to her, and she was gone. I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Lodz, 1944, when they liquidated the ghetto. They took first the people to a place, and then from there, they send them out. So I'd been in-- in-- already at the place. We go in. They gave us each a bread, and a piece of margarine, something unusual. I had a knife with me. And we go in. We didn't know where we're going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it winter or summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was in-- month of-- no, it was spring. It wasn't bad. It wasn't cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: What clothing did you have with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothings-- whatever we could grab. And we didn't have much, that I can tell you. But-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] --when we arrived to this place, like I said, we get a bread and margarine. I had a knife with us, with all the little belongings. We go on the train. I never saw that auntie any more. And ironic, they were in ghetto with their son. They had only one son. The son was my age. They were in ghetto-- in Lodz ghetto-- all together. And then, come to Auschwitz, they were separated. I didn't see my uncle. I didn't see my cousin. I just saw my auntie. I don't know. She disappeared so quick, or maybe they took her. I-- I-- this, I don't really remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this auntie?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's sister before my mother. My mother was the youngest, and this-- and this was one before her. She had five sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we going on the train. We had bread. We have a-- a knife and margarine. We going. We sitting down and-- in that open carts, you know, where the animals are transferred. How many people are on this train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like the herrings. I couldn't tell you-- like the herrings. Finally, as we go into Auschwitz with that train-- before Auschwitz, you could see men in their striped suits and the-- the little hats. And you could see zombies-- living zombies.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my brother saw this. He said to me, you see? You wanted to volunteer. He had-- Where did you think you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you think you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know it! Till we came into Auschwitz, we didn't know what's going on. I-- we knew they taken Yidden. They are killing them. They bury them alive. But we didn't know that something like this can happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as my brother sees this, he said to me, you wanted to volunteer. He had a pair of beautiful boots. He took the knife, and he cut off the-- the tops. He said, at least, maybe, I'll be able to save my shoes. As we arrived to Auschwitz, I said to my brother, let's kiss goodbye. He said-- this I'll never forget-- he took his hand like this. He said, go. You wanted to volunteer. You see where we're coming? And if this little brother wouldn't survive, I would cry all my life. So we're coming down to Auschwitz. What happened? I said goodbye. My brother didn't want to say goodbye to me. He left. And we are segregated.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- and then, in Auschwitz, I see a cousin of mines stays on my left hand. And she said-- so she was going [NON-ENGLISH], and I was gone the last.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put us in quarantine, in Auschwitz, for 10 days. And the people which were there-- these people already knew what's going on. And they told us. What was a big tragedy-- my sister found out that I am here. She came running to my barrack. She said, I'm going to slip myself in-- that we can be together-- clutching in her hand a little piece of bread for me-- my sister. We've been so close at home, in such a wonderful family. You knows-- arguing? Nothing!

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister clutches a piece of bread. She gives me that piece of bread and just said, tomorrow morning, I slip in-- in your barrack. And we're going to be together. It turned out that's this morning never came. I-- we got in quarantine. They gave us a dress-- no clothes more than a dress-- shaved off. That's what I have to tell you. As we arrived to Auschwitz, and we stayed-- and then we stayed in line, I was always conscious of myself-- how I look. So I clutched in my hand a little mirror and a little comb. As I saw in front of me the lady-- all the girls had their heads shaved off. And we walked in a carpet of hair-- human hair. I threw-- I threw that comb and that mirror in the-- in the hair. I thought to myself, you don't need a mirror here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we've been-- after that, we've been in the barracks maybe a day or two. They selected us again. And they put us in in a big space in Auschwitz, under the sky-- the-- the skies.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was already warm. The sun was scorching us. And they gave us food. They gave us food in a one big container-- no spoons-- no nothing. Everybody start fighting about it. The soup was more on the floor, on the ground, than it was to consume. I-- I stayed and said to myself, that low, I'm already not. People were fighting for the food. As I said, there are no spoons here-- in Polish-- looks that it was a volksdeutsche-- and he understood me. And he gave me one in my face-- a big-- in my face. All right. We stayed there quarantined under the sun. The bullets are-- are flying over us, because this was already 1944. The Americans and the Russians-- everybody was already beating Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I stay under the sun, and I just dozed off, I couldn't fight for any food. I hear a voice saying [NON-ENGLISH]. I recognize my girlfriend's voice. But she was taken to Auschwitz long, long before us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, Etta! She lives, thank God. Etta! She said, don't worry. I'll bring you some water today. I'll bring you some bread. She was over there a big-- a big shot in the-- in the-- in Auschwitz. My brother, they took to take care of the horses in the stables-- a 12-year-old little boy. He has now his hands crippled from arthritis. And he slept with the horse, and he stayed there. He had a little food more, because he was so little that the-- the soldiers came in, and they called him [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] means small in German. And when the war was almost to end, they said to my brother, [GERMAN]. That means, we lost the war. But we-- we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe those barracks that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barracks? Dirty-- on the-- on-- in Auschwitz-- dirty, sleeping on the ground-- we had one blanket. Everybody pulled. And it was cold at night. But thank God I wasn't more in Auschwitz than 10 days. I was quarantined.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to Etta, Helen is here-- my sister. She said, yes. She was here sitting right where you're sitting. She was sended out to-- from Auschwitz-- to a labor camp. So I was already relieved that my sister is alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother tells me now-- I was in Israel last year, in June. We had a reunion-- my sister, my brother, my brother-in-law, and my sister-in-law. We had a wonderful time. My son sent me with a maid to take care of me. And I was two weeks in Israel. We had a wonderful reunion.

speaker: interviewer
text: But a lot of people came which lived in our city. We've been all friends, everybody-- friends-- young people. And my brother, as we sitting and talking, he said to me, you remember? You volunteered to go to Auschwitz. And right away his tears-- you can't talk with my brother about the camp. He is in tears right away. So in Ozorkow ghetto-- in Lodz ghetto, when we got with this transport, they gave us, again, only a dress. And they gave us again a bread and a-- and margarine. And we went.

speaker: interviewee
text: Luckily, I saw my brother go in for-- they delouse you. That means they-- they bathe you. You got more lice from them than you had-- you had before. We had plenty of lice. And my-- I saw my brother going back with the transport. And he wore his boots-- the halves-- what he cut. Oh, I was relieved that he is alive. He'll go to a-- a working camp. Some day, maybe God willing, we'll see each other. My-- this friend of mines, which brought me the water every day, we stayed over there quite a few days under the clear skies. And she said to me, don't cry about Helen. She was here yesterday-- here. She was sent to a labor camp. So I was relieved. Then my brother tells me now that he saw my father in Birkenau. This was Auschwitz-- a little outskirt. He saw my father. My-- he said he didn't recognize my father. in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: My-- my brother, now in Israel, he told me. I never knew. He said, I didn't recognize him. He didn't recognize me. But I start talking about mommy-- mommy died, and things like that. He will start crying. What could he say? So we are-- we are sitting on that place where we've been-- should be disputed. The-- no. Was that the last time your brother saw your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or that you heard what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- yes. I never saw my father since he stepped out from Lodz ghetto. I never saw my father. So my-- we've been in in that quarantine. All right, thank God. They're loading us again on-- on the cars. I guess we're going to a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know why they didn't keep you at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: They needed labor! They needed workers. I was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you think? We'd-- I'd been making parts to-- to planes. The machine was-- was as big as this room. I-- I made the parts, and I packed them in sawdust, and wired the box. When you did something wrong-- you do sabotage-- you paid the consequences. When did you make these parts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now-- I'm telling you now. As we-- they loaded us, we're coming down to a labor camp. Every time at night, as we stepped out at those cars, I smell, you know, a-- a nice country smell. And I was relieved.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said to the other girls, you see? We're coming out to a village. And maybe, God willing, we'll survive. And all the girls were saying, yeah. Look. You feel it. There was a man, commander [NON-ENGLISH]. What's his name? I don't remember. It was a man, commander [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us in to-- this was Mittelsteine. They took us in to a new barracks. There were not yet straws in the-- in the-- in the bunk beds. There were three-- four flights. And-- and it was paradise from the Lodz ghetto. There was a table. There was chairs. There were dishes for each-- a cup for each of one. And me, I was lucky. As we stay in the line, the-- the-- the man-- commander [NON-ENGLISH] said to me, [NON-ENGLISH]. That means, you are taking care of your room. You must see in the morning, before the girls leave for work-- the beds have to be cleaned. The table has to be cleaned. They've been going for inspection-- stubenalteste.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I'm stubenalteste. Every time they're giving the rations, I got double bread and got double soups. And what did I do? I didn't have my sister. I cried my eyes out. My sister could benefit from that. So I had two girls, which we been in Lodz ghetto together. We lived in the same yard-- Handke and Topje. Every-- every night, I came home, and I-- I fed them. I had what to eat! I got food. So it was nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked a few months. In front of me, at-- in this factory, it was-- there were all kind of nationalities, but not on the guards like ours-- with electric wires. They were-- they were free. They came to work. After work, they could go to the village. They could buy some things. And they-- so in front of me worked a Polish girl. We start talking Polish. She was over there longer than I. As we arrived, I start talking to her in Polish, and, oh, oh! She's all happy. Every night, she went home, they-- in the morning she made an excuse to come to my machine. There was staying a-- a-- a cabinet with tools to-- to screw the things. They taught us. And every morning, she came around. She brought me cooked potatoes and the peels. She brought me eggs. She brought me fruit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what is-- was it? In exchange of that, I was sewing for her. I made her bras. I was sitting and sewing in-- in the beds. So now I tell you. This comes to bad part.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked. And it was paradise. Because I couldn't eat that much, I came home, I fed those two girls-- those two friends. And then there was over here, a girl, [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't think she was more than 14 years old. I fed her, too. First slept Handke, Topje, me, and [NON-ENGLISH]. I came home home in the evening-- the rich mother came with food. Yeah! It was. I worked in this shop a few months. I was happy. The shiksa-- the Polka had already a baby from over there. They lived in the camps. And I was sewing for her and made her aprons-- everything by hand in the evening, when I came home. All of a sudden, I took sick-- but very ill. Every night, when I came back from work, I was burning up. And Handke and Topje said, why don't you go in to the Revier. That means-- Revier means a-- a sick room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you go in and take your temperature? You're killing yourself. I said, oh, how can I leave this job? We live-- we all live from it! So they-- it was going-- in the morning, I didn't have any temperature. I went to the shop to work. Finally, one morning, I just couldn't make it. And Handke and Topje brought me in to the sick-- to that sick place.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I went there, she-- she took-- there was a nurse-- no doctor-- a Hungarian girl. She was a Jew, but she was a anti-- anti-Semite. You know, the Hungarians are very assimilated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said, go to bed in the sick room. Still on the floor, I laid down. I got sicker and sicker. Finally, she decided that I have a pneumonia and pleurisy in my right lung, which I was almost on the deathbed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was lucky in this camp. Sitting in my bed, when I got a little better, I was mending stockings for the German Aufseherinnen. I was making a print. You should see it-- embroidered by hand. And this is what saved me. Even the Germans, after I was so ill, they brought me food. They did-- the Aufseherinnen. That was the-- the girls. And God willing, I got well. All of a sudden, one evening, I was-- it must have been the crisis-- I stay in bed. And the whole camp say, Bluma is going to be the first victim of this. Nobody died in this camp. Bluma is going to be the first victim in this camp to die. All of a sudden, I feel a prick in my shoulder. I got some kind of injection. And the next day, I was better. So I stayed another week in the sick room. One morning, I hear that the Jews-- commander [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know we had a Jewish girl, and we had a German. Both of them were watching us. I hear Helen say, inspection comes from Auschwitz. You know, they came and they segregated. Especially from the sick room, they took-- they came a few times a year. As I hear this, I jumped down from my bed, from my-- on the second-- second bunk I was sleeping. I jumped down. And I said, I'm not sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I walked around in the-- in that sick room. A German comes in. He takes my pulse. And he said, she should stay a little bit longer here. Can you imagine this?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they left, everybody start kissing me. You see, you-- I don't know what, but you have life. You-- you had. And I-- I was lucky. The girls-- which-- the Jewish girls, which I loved, they're working in the-- in the kitchens where we-- they cooked our food. They used to come in, and they used to give me so much food I couldn't even-- I couldn't even eat!

speaker: interviewer
text: When I was so very ill, Topje and Handke came behind the window. They didn't let in nobody in the sick room. She said, you want something to eat? I said, take my bread, take my everything. If you can, bring me an apple. So God willing, I-- I'm-- I'm safe from the Germans for a while. My temperature doesn't comes down. The [NON-ENGLISH]-- that Hungarian nurse, comes in, and he punctures my lung-- if I have pleurisy. She didn't find nothing. After she had done, she said to me, you know what? For the first time in my life I did this. I said to myself, I hope I'm-- I'm not scarred for life. Which I am. My right lung is very weak, and I have to be very careful. But this healed, you see? But you can see it that I had pneumonia. So we've been in-- in Mittelsteine. I would already walking around a little bit. Is Mittelsteine in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany. This was the labor camp. I've been there after-- just before the war ended. They-- they came in, and they start splitting the camp in 1/2. One-- one group is going to other camp. And we are going-- and we are going to another labor camp. As we arrived in this labor camp, I'm telling you, there was a dead place.
